meta.title: OpenGL Rendering Pipeline — LWJGL / Java
meta.description: Structured documentation of a standard OpenGL rendering pipeline implemented in Java using LWJGL.

core_functions.name: glfwInit
core_functions.returns: boolean — true if GLFW initialized successfully
core_functions.description: Initializes the GLFW library. Must be the very first GLFW call.

core_functions.name: glfwWindowHint
core_functions.description: Configures properties for the next window to be created. Must be called before glfwCreateWindow.

parameters.name: hint
parameters.description: The window property to configure.

alternatives.use_when: Setting the major OpenGL version (e.g. 3 for OpenGL 3.3)
Setting the minor OpenGL version (e.g. 3 for OpenGL 3.3)
Selecting Core vs Compatibility profile
Required on macOS to allow forward-compatible context
Allow or prevent the user from resizing the window
Control whether the window is visible on creation

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to assign to the hint.

alternatives.use_when: Use with GLFW_OPENGL_PROFILE — standard choice for modern OpenGL
Use with GLFW_OPENGL_PROFILE — allows deprecated features; avoid for new projects
Boolean enable value (e.g. GLFW_RESIZABLE, GLFW_OPENGL_FORWARD_COMPAT)
Boolean disable value

parameters.name: monitor

Fullscreen on the primary monitor

parameters.name: window

core_functions.name: glfwSwapInterval
core_functions.description: Controls VSync. Sets how many monitor refreshes to wait before swapping buffers.

parameters.name: interval

alternatives.use_when: VSync OFF — uncapped frame rate, useful for benchmarking
VSync ON — syncs to monitor refresh rate (default/recommended)

core_functions.name: glfwShowWindow

parameters.name: window

supporting_functions.name: glfwSetErrorCallback
supporting_functions.returns: GLFWErrorCallback — the previously set callback (can be null)
supporting_functions.description: Registers a callback that fires whenever GLFW encounters an error. Install before glfwInit to catch init errors.

parameters.name: cbfun
parameters.description: The error handler to register. Use GLFWErrorCallback.createPrint(System.err) for instant console logging.

alternatives.use_when: Print all GLFW errors to stderr — recommended during development
Throw a Java exception on any GLFW error — useful for strict error handling
Remove/clear a previously installed callback

supporting_functions.example_call: glfwSetErrorCallback(GLFWErrorCallback.createPrint(System.err));

supporting_functions.name: glfwSetKeyCallback
supporting_functions.description: Registers a keyboard input callback. Fires once per key press, repeat, or release event.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: The window to attach the callback to.

parameters.name: cbfun
parameters.type: GLFWKeyCallbackI (lambda: window, key, scancode, action, mods)
parameters.description: Lambda or method reference receiving: window handle, GLFW_KEY_* constant, OS scancode, GLFW_PRESS/RELEASE/REPEAT, modifier bitmask.
parameters.value: (win, key, scancode, action, mods) -> { if (key == GLFW_KEY_ESCAPE && action == GLFW_PRESS) glfwSetWindowShouldClose(win, true); }

supporting_functions.name: glfwSetFramebufferSizeCallback
supporting_functions.description: Fires whenever the framebuffer is resized. Use to call glViewport with the new dimensions so rendering scales correctly.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: The window to monitor for resize events.

parameters.name: cbfun
parameters.description: Lambda receiving the new framebuffer width and height in pixels.

supporting_functions.example_call: glfwSetFramebufferSizeCallback(window, (win, w, h) -> glViewport(0, 0, w, h));

supporting_functions.name: glfwSetCursorPosCallback
supporting_functions.description: Fires whenever the mouse cursor moves. Use for camera look/rotation based on mouse delta.

parameters.name: cbfun

supporting_functions.name: glfwSetInputMode

supporting_functions.description: Controls input capture behaviour. Use to hide and lock the cursor for first-person camera control.

supporting_functions.signature: void glfwSetWindowPos(long window, int xpos, int ypos)

supporting_functions.name: glfwGetTime

supporting_functions.description: Returns high-resolution elapsed time. Use for delta time calculation, animations, and frame timing.

execution_order.reason: Install error handler first — catches any GLFW errors that occur during init
Initialize GLFW library — must be the very first GLFW call after the error callback
Set OpenGL major version before window creation — hints are consumed by glfwCreateWindow
Set OpenGL minor version — together with step 3 targets OpenGL 3.3
Select Core profile — disables deprecated fixed-function features
Hide window initially — allows positioning before showing, avoids window flash
Allow window resize — set any remaining window property hints before creation
Create the OS window and OpenGL context — all hints apply here and are consumed

execution_order.call: if (window == NULL) throw new RuntimeException("Window creation failed")
execution_order.reason: Null-check the handle immediately — glfwCreateWindow returns NULL on failure
Register input callbacks after window exists but before the context is made current
Register resize callback — ensures glViewport updates when window is resized
Register mouse callback — set all callbacks before entering the render loop
Query monitor resolution — used to centre the window on screen
Centre window on screen — must be done before showing the window
Activate OpenGL context on this thread — required before any GL call or GL.createCapabilities
Enable VSync — must be called after context is current, before the render loop
Make window visible — show only after context is ready and window is positioned

code_example.description: Creates the GLFW window and OpenGL context. This is the very first code that runs — nothing else works until this completes.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 1 is the absolute entry point of the entire pipeline. Nothing — no GL call, no shader, no buffer, no texture — can exist before this phase completes. It is responsible for creating the OS window, the OpenGL context, and the GLFW event system that all other phases depend on.
integration_context.comes_after: Nothing. This is the first phase. It is called directly from main() with no prerequisites.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 2 (GL Context Initialization) — GL.createCapabilities() must be the very next call after glfwMakeContextCurrent(). Phase 3 through 11 are all blocked until this phase succeeds. If glfwCreateWindow returns NULL or glfwInit fails, the engine must abort immediately.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling any GLFW function before glfwInit()
common_mistakes.symptom: JVM crash or silent failure with no error message
common_mistakes.fix: glfwInit() must be the first GLFW call. Install the error callback before it with glfwSetErrorCallback() to catch the failure message.

common_mistakes.trap: Not checking the return value of glfwCreateWindow()
common_mistakes.symptom: NullPointerException or access violation on the next GLFW call — window handle is 0 (NULL)
common_mistakes.fix: Always null-check immediately: if (window == NULL) throw new RuntimeException(). glfwCreateWindow returns NULL silently on failure — the error callback will have the reason.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling GL functions before glfwMakeContextCurrent()
common_mistakes.symptom: UnsatisfiedLinkError or NullPointerException inside GL.createCapabilities() — no context is active on the thread
common_mistakes.fix: glfwMakeContextCurrent(window) must be called before GL.createCapabilities() and before any glXxx call. The context is thread-local — if you move rendering to another thread, call makeContextCurrent there too.

common_mistakes.trap: Setting glfwWindowHint() after glfwCreateWindow()
common_mistakes.symptom: Hints are silently ignored — window is created with default settings, wrong OpenGL version or profile
common_mistakes.fix: All glfwWindowHint() calls must come before glfwCreateWindow(). Hints are consumed at creation time and have no effect afterward.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting GLFW_OPENGL_PROFILE hint on macOS
common_mistakes.symptom: OpenGL 2.1 context created instead of 3.3 core — shader compilation fails with #version 330 unsupported
common_mistakes.fix: On macOS, also add glfwWindowHint(GLFW_OPENGL_FORWARD_COMPAT, GLFW_TRUE) alongside GLFW_OPENGL_CORE_PROFILE. Without it, macOS silently downgrades the context version.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glfwSwapInterval() before glfwMakeContextCurrent()
common_mistakes.symptom: VSync has no effect — frame rate is uncapped regardless of the interval value
common_mistakes.fix: glfwSwapInterval() only applies to the current context. Call it after glfwMakeContextCurrent(window), not before.

common_mistakes.trap: Not registering glfwSetFramebufferSizeCallback()
common_mistakes.symptom: Rendering stretches or squashes when the window is resized — viewport stays at original size
common_mistakes.fix: Register the callback in init() and call glViewport(0, 0, w, h) inside it. Without this, the viewport never updates and the aspect ratio breaks on resize.

common_mistakes.trap: Not freeing GLFW callbacks before glfwDestroyWindow()
common_mistakes.symptom: Native memory leak — no visible crash but memory grows over multiple engine restarts or scene reloads
common_mistakes.fix: Call Callbacks.glfwFreeCallbacks(window) before glfwDestroyWindow(window). Each registered callback holds a native function pointer that the GC cannot reclaim.

core_functions.name: GL.createCapabilities
core_functions.returns: GLCapabilities — object describing all available GL extensions and version support
core_functions.description: Detects and loads all OpenGL function pointers for the current context. Must be called once after glfwMakeContextCurrent, before any GL call.

core_functions.name: glViewport
core_functions.signature: void glViewport(int x, int y, int width, int height)
core_functions.description: Defines the pixel rectangle on the framebuffer that OpenGL renders into.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Left edge of the viewport in pixels from the bottom-left of the window.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Bottom edge of the viewport in pixels from the bottom-left of the window.

parameters.name: width
parameters.description: Width of the viewport in pixels. Typically equals window width.

parameters.name: height
parameters.description: Height of the viewport in pixels. Typically equals window height.

core_functions.name: glClearColor
core_functions.signature: void glClearColor(float red, float green, float blue, float alpha)
core_functions.description: Sets the color used to clear the color buffer at the start of each frame. Values are in [0.0, 1.0] range.

parameters.name: red
parameters.description: Red channel of the clear color.

parameters.name: green
parameters.description: Green channel of the clear color.

parameters.name: blue
parameters.description: Blue channel of the clear color.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description: Alpha channel of the clear color. Use 1.0f for fully opaque.

core_functions.name: glEnable
core_functions.description: Activates a named OpenGL capability (e.g. depth testing, blending, culling). Disabled by default unless enabled here.

parameters.name: cap
parameters.description: The capability to enable.

alternatives.use_when: Enable depth testing — required for correct 3D occlusion
Enable alpha blending for transparent objects
Enable back-face culling to skip rendering hidden faces
Enable MSAA anti-aliasing if the context was created with multisampling

supporting_functions.name: glDisable
supporting_functions.description: Deactivates a named OpenGL capability. Use to temporarily toggle depth testing, blending, or culling mid-frame.

parameters.name: cap
parameters.description: The capability to disable. Must be the same constant used with glEnable.

alternatives.use_when: Disable depth testing for 2D/UI overlay rendering on top of 3D scene
Disable blending after rendering transparent objects to restore opaque pass state
Disable culling for double-sided geometry like foliage or glass

supporting_functions.name: glGetString
supporting_functions.returns: String — the requested driver/version string (null if name is invalid)
supporting_functions.description: Queries a human-readable string about the current OpenGL context. Use at startup to log driver info and verify version.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Which string to retrieve.

alternatives.use_when: Full OpenGL version string including driver version — log at startup to verify context
GPU vendor name (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel) — useful for driver-specific workarounds
GPU model name — log for support/debug purposes
GLSL version supported — must match #version directive in shaders

supporting_functions.example_call:
System.out.println("OpenGL: " + glGetString(GL_VERSION));
System.out.println("GLSL:   " + glGetString(GL_SHADING_LANGUAGE_VERSION));

supporting_functions.name: glGetIntegerv
supporting_functions.returns: void — result written into the IntBuffer
supporting_functions.description: Queries an integer state value from the current OpenGL context. Use to check limits like max texture units or max UBO size.

parameters.name: pname
parameters.description: The state parameter to query.

alternatives.use_when: Max texture units available in fragment shader — typically 16+
Max vertex attributes — minimum 16 guaranteed by spec
Max bytes per uniform buffer block
Total texture units across all shader stages

parameters.name: params
parameters.type: IntBuffer (user value — direct NIO buffer of at least size 1)
parameters.description: Buffer that receives the queried integer value(s).

supporting_functions.name: glPolygonMode
supporting_functions.description: Sets the rasterization mode for polygons. Switch to GL_LINE for wireframe debug rendering without changing geometry.

parameters.name: face
parameters.description: Which faces the mode applies to. Always use GL_FRONT_AND_BACK in core profile.

alternatives.use_when: Apply to all faces — only valid option in core profile

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: How polygons are rasterized.

alternatives.use_when: Normal solid filled rendering — default
Wireframe mode — draws only triangle edges, useful for mesh debugging
Point mode — renders only vertices, useful for vertex debug visualization

supporting_functions.example_call:
glPolygonMode(GL_FRONT_AND_BACK, GL_LINE); // wireframe debug
glPolygonMode(GL_FRONT_AND_BACK, GL_FILL); // restore

supporting_functions.name: glScissor
supporting_functions.signature: void glScissor(int x, int y, int width, int height)
supporting_functions.description: Restricts rendering to a rectangular pixel region. Requires glEnable(GL_SCISSOR_TEST). Useful for UI clipping and partial clears.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Left edge of the scissor rectangle in window coordinates.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Bottom edge of the scissor rectangle. Note: OpenGL Y=0 is the bottom of the window.

parameters.name: width
parameters.description: Width of the scissor rectangle.

parameters.name: height
parameters.description: Height of the scissor rectangle.

supporting_functions.name: glLineWidth
supporting_functions.description: Sets the width of rasterized lines in pixels. Only effective for GL_LINES draw calls and GL_LINE polygon mode.

parameters.name: width
parameters.description: Line width in pixels. Values above 1.0f may not be supported on all drivers in core profile.

supporting_functions.example_call: glLineWidth(2.0f);

execution_order.reason: Load all OpenGL function pointers for the current context — must be the very first GL call, immediately after glfwMakeContextCurrent
Log the actual OpenGL version granted by the driver — verify it matches what was requested in hints
Log the GLSL version — must match the #version directive used in all shader source files
Define the rendering rectangle within the window — must be set before any draw calls
Set the background clear color — applied every frame when glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT) is called
Activate depth testing — required for correct 3D occlusion; set once during init
Set depth comparison rule — GL_LESS is the default but explicit is safer and clearer
Activate back-face culling — discard hidden faces early for performance
Specify which faces to cull — GL_BACK discards faces pointing away from the camera
Define front-face winding order — GL_CCW matches most 3D exporters and OpenGL convention
Explicitly set fill mode — makes switching to GL_LINE wireframe debug mode and back predictable

code_example.location: Engine.java → init() method, immediately after glfwMakeContextCurrent(window)
code_example.description: Loads all OpenGL function pointers and sets the default GL state. Must run before any GL call and before shaders, buffers, or textures are created.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 2 is the bridge between the GLFW window and the rest of the OpenGL pipeline. It activates the GL function pointer table and establishes all default rendering state that every subsequent phase depends on. Without GL.createCapabilities(), every GL call silently crashes or does nothing. Without the default state set here (depth test, cull face, viewport), rendering produces incorrect or invisible output even if shaders and meshes are correct.
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 1 (Window & Context Creation) — glfwMakeContextCurrent(window) must have been called before GL.createCapabilities(). This phase runs in the same init() block, immediately after Phase 1 completes.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 3 (Shader System) — shaders can only be compiled after GL capabilities are loaded. Phase 4 (Mesh Setup) — VAOs and VBOs require a live GL context with capabilities loaded. Phase 5 (Texture Loading) — glGenTextures and glTexImage2D are only available after GL.createCapabilities(). In short, every remaining phase depends on this one completing successfully.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling any glXxx function before GL.createCapabilities()
common_mistakes.symptom: NullPointerException or UnsatisfiedLinkError deep inside LWJGL — the function pointer table is empty
common_mistakes.fix: GL.createCapabilities() must be the absolute first GL call after glfwMakeContextCurrent(). No exceptions — not even glGetString or glViewport are safe before it.

common_mistakes.trap: Never calling glViewport() after context creation
common_mistakes.symptom: Rendering appears in only a portion of the window, or is clipped — default viewport may be 0x0 on some drivers
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glViewport(0, 0, width, height) explicitly after GL.createCapabilities(). Do not rely on the driver initialising it to the window size.

common_mistakes.trap: Not updating glViewport() when the window is resized
common_mistakes.symptom: Scene stretches or compresses on resize — rendering region stays at original dimensions
common_mistakes.fix: Call glViewport(0, 0, newWidth, newHeight) inside the glfwSetFramebufferSizeCallback registered in Phase 1. Also rebuild the projection matrix with the new aspect ratio.

common_mistakes.trap: Enabling GL_DEPTH_TEST but forgetting GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT in glClear()
common_mistakes.symptom: Depth buffer fills with values from the previous frame — geometry from the last frame bleeds through current geometry, z-fighting on every frame
common_mistakes.fix: Always pass GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT to glClear() when depth testing is active. Clearing color alone leaves the depth buffer dirty.

common_mistakes.trap: Assuming GL capabilities or default state is consistent across drivers
common_mistakes.symptom: Works on one machine, black screen or crash on another — driver initialises different defaults
common_mistakes.fix: Always set all GL state explicitly: glDepthFunc, glCullFace, glFrontFace, glPolygonMode. Never rely on defaults being consistent across GPU vendors or operating systems.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling GL.createCapabilities() on the wrong thread
common_mistakes.symptom: GL function pointers load successfully but all GL calls crash or produce no output — context is current on a different thread
common_mistakes.fix: The OpenGL context is thread-local. GL.createCapabilities() and all GL calls must run on the same thread that called glfwMakeContextCurrent(). Never share a context across threads without explicit transfer.

common_mistakes.trap: Ignoring the output of glGetString(GL_VERSION) at startup
common_mistakes.symptom: Shader compilation fails with version mismatch — driver granted OpenGL 2.1 instead of 3.3 with no obvious error
common_mistakes.fix: Always log glGetString(GL_VERSION) and glGetString(GL_SHADING_LANGUAGE_VERSION) immediately after GL.createCapabilities(). If the version is lower than requested, the window hints were not applied correctly.

core_functions.name: glCreateShader
core_functions.returns: int — shader object ID (0 if creation failed)
core_functions.description: Creates an empty shader object of the specified stage type.

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: The pipeline stage this shader belongs to.

alternatives.use_when: Processing vertex positions — always required
Computing pixel/fragment color — always required
Optional stage between vertex and fragment for generating extra geometry
General-purpose GPU compute, not part of the rasterization pipeline

core_functions.name: glShaderSource
core_functions.description: Uploads GLSL source code string(s) into the shader object.

parameters.name: shader
parameters.type: int (user value — shader ID from glCreateShader)
parameters.description: The shader object to attach source code to.

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: The GLSL source code. LWJGL accepts a raw Java String.

core_functions.example_call: glShaderSource(vertexShader, "#version 330 core\n" + "layout(location=0) in vec3 aPos;\n" + "void main(){ gl_Position = vec4(aPos, 1.0); }");

core_functions.name: glCompileShader
core_functions.description: Compiles the GLSL source code previously loaded into the shader object.

parameters.name: shader

parameters.name: shader
parameters.description: The shader object to query.

parameters.name: pname
parameters.description: The parameter to retrieve.

Query which pipeline stage this shader is
Get the length of the error log before calling glGetShaderInfoLog

core_functions.name: glGetShaderInfoLog
core_functions.returns: String — compiler error/warning messages (empty string if none)

core_functions.description: Creates an empty shader program object that will hold linked shader stages.

parameters.name: program

core_functions.name: glGetProgrami
core_functions.description: Queries a parameter of a shader program. Used to check whether linking succeeded.

parameters.name: program
parameters.description: The shader program to query.

parameters.name: pname
parameters.description: The parameter to retrieve.

Check if program is valid for the current GL state (after glValidateProgram)
Query how many active uniforms the program has

core_functions.name: glGetProgramInfoLog
core_functions.description: Retrieves the linker error log. Call after glLinkProgram when GL_LINK_STATUS is GL_FALSE.

parameters.name: program
parameters.description: The program whose log to retrieve.

core_functions.name: glUseProgram
core_functions.description: Activates a shader program for all subsequent draw calls.

parameters.name: program
parameters.description: The shader program to activate. Pass 0 to unbind any active program.

alternatives.use_when: Activate your compiled program before a draw call
Unbind / deactivate all shader programs

core_functions.name: glDeleteShader
core_functions.description: Frees a shader object after it has been linked into a program. The program retains the compiled code.

parameters.name: shader
parameters.description: The shader object to delete.

supporting_functions.name: glValidateProgram
supporting_functions.description: Validates a linked shader program against the current OpenGL state. Result readable via glGetProgrami(GL_VALIDATE_STATUS). Use during debug only — not in production render loops.

parameters.name: program
parameters.type: int (user value — linked program ID from glCreateProgram)
parameters.description: The shader program to validate.

supporting_functions.name: glDetachShader
supporting_functions.description: Detaches a shader object from a program after linking. Allows the shader object to be fully freed when deleted. Optional but recommended for clean resource management.

parameters.name: program
parameters.description: The program to detach the shader from.

parameters.name: shader
parameters.description: The shader object to detach.

supporting_functions.example_call:
glDetachShader(shaderProgram, vertexShader);
glDetachShader(shaderProgram, fragmentShader);

supporting_functions.name: glGetAttribLocation
supporting_functions.returns: int — attribute location index (-1 if not found or inactive)
supporting_functions.description: Queries the location of a named vertex attribute in the shader program. Alternative to using layout(location=N) in GLSL when explicit locations are not set.

parameters.name: program
parameters.description: The shader program to query.

parameters.name: name
parameters.type: CharSequence (user value — exact GLSL attribute name)
parameters.description: The name of the vertex attribute as declared in the vertex shader. Case-sensitive.

supporting_functions.example_call:
int posLoc = glGetAttribLocation(shaderProgram, "aPos");
glVertexAttribPointer(posLoc, 3, GL_FLOAT, false, 3 * Float.BYTES, 0L);

supporting_functions.name: glBindAttribLocation
supporting_functions.signature: void glBindAttribLocation(int program, int index, CharSequence name)
supporting_functions.description: Explicitly assigns a location index to a named vertex attribute before linking. An alternative to layout(location=N) in GLSL — must be called before glLinkProgram.

parameters.name: program
parameters.type: int (user value — program ID, before linking)
parameters.description: The program to assign the attribute location in.

parameters.name: index
parameters.type: int (user value — attribute slot 0 to GL_MAX_VERTEX_ATTRIBS-1)
parameters.description: The location index to assign. Must match the index used in glVertexAttribPointer.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The vertex attribute name as declared in the vertex shader source.

supporting_functions.example_call:
glBindAttribLocation(shaderProgram, 0, "aPos"); // before glLinkProgram
glLinkProgram(shaderProgram);

execution_order.reason: Load vertex shader GLSL source from disk into a Java String before any GL shader calls
Load fragment shader source — both sources must be ready before creating shader objects
Create an empty vertex shader object — returns an ID to attach source to
Upload GLSL source into the vertex shader object — must happen before compile
Compile the vertex shader source on the GPU driver
Check compile result immediately — do not proceed to linking if compilation failed
Print the compiler error log — only meaningful when GL_COMPILE_STATUS is GL_FALSE
Create an empty fragment shader object — same pattern as vertex shader
Upload fragment shader GLSL source into the shader object
Compile the fragment shader — each shader stage compiled independently
Check fragment compile result — abort and log if it failed
Print fragment compiler errors — only call when GL_COMPILE_STATUS is GL_FALSE
Create an empty program object to link shader stages into — done after both shaders compile successfully
Attach compiled vertex shader to the program — must be attached before linking
Attach compiled fragment shader to the program — both stages required for a complete pipeline
Link all attached shaders into a complete GPU program — resolves inter-stage variable connections
Check link result immediately — a program can compile but still fail to link
Print linker errors — only call when GL_LINK_STATUS is GL_FALSE
Detach vertex shader from program — program retains compiled bytecode, object can now be fully freed
Detach fragment shader — must detach before delete to allow full GPU memory reclaim
Free the vertex shader object — no longer needed once linked into the program
Free the fragment shader object — cleanup immediately after linking, not at shutdown
Activate the linked program — required before querying uniform locations or issuing draw calls

code_example.location: ShaderProgram.java → standalone class, called from Engine.java → init()
code_example.description: Encapsulates the full compile → link → validate → cache uniforms pipeline. Separating shader logic into its own class keeps Engine.java clean and makes hot-reloading shaders easier later.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 3 is the programmable core of the pipeline. Every pixel drawn by the GPU passes through a shader program created here. Without a linked and active shader program, draw calls produce no output — the GPU has no instructions on how to process vertices or compute fragment colors. The shader program is also the integration point for Phase 6 (matrices) and Phase 7 (uniforms) — both phases write data into locations that only exist inside a linked program.
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 2 (GL Context Initialization) — GL.createCapabilities() must have loaded all GL function pointers before glCreateShader or glCreateProgram can be called. glViewport and default GL state must also be set so the first draw call after shader activation renders correctly.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 4 (Mesh Setup) — vertex attribute locations defined in the vertex shader (layout(location=N)) must be known before glVertexAttribPointer is configured, so attribute indices match between shader and VAO. Phase 7 (Uniform Management) — glGetUniformLocation can only be called after glLinkProgram succeeds. Phase 8 (Render Loop) — glUseProgram must be called each frame before any draw call, making the shader the first thing activated at the start of each render pass.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glGetUniformLocation() inside the render loop
common_mistakes.symptom: Severe frame rate drop — glGetUniformLocation is a slow driver query that stalls the GPU pipeline when called thousands of times per second
common_mistakes.fix: Query all uniform locations once after glLinkProgram() and store them in fields. Only use the cached integer locations inside the render loop.

common_mistakes.trap: Not checking GL_COMPILE_STATUS after glCompileShader()
common_mistakes.symptom: glLinkProgram silently fails or links a broken program — draw calls produce no output with no obvious error
common_mistakes.fix: Always check glGetShaderi(shader, GL_COMPILE_STATUS) immediately after glCompileShader(). If GL_FALSE, print glGetShaderInfoLog() and abort. Never proceed to linking a shader that failed to compile.

common_mistakes.trap: Not checking GL_LINK_STATUS after glLinkProgram()
common_mistakes.symptom: glUseProgram silently activates an invalid program — all draw calls produce black output or nothing
common_mistakes.fix: Always check glGetProgrami(program, GL_LINK_STATUS) immediately after glLinkProgram(). If GL_FALSE, print glGetProgramInfoLog() and abort. A shader can compile successfully but still fail to link.

common_mistakes.trap: Mismatched attribute location between VAO and vertex shader
common_mistakes.symptom: Geometry renders as garbage, scrambled positions, or completely invisible — GPU reads wrong bytes as vertex data
common_mistakes.fix: The index in glVertexAttribPointer(index, ...) must exactly match layout(location=index) in the GLSL vertex shader. Use explicit layout qualifiers in GLSL rather than relying on glGetAttribLocation to avoid mismatches.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glUseProgram() with a program that failed to link
common_mistakes.symptom: Undefined rendering behaviour — black screen, driver warnings, or crash depending on the GPU vendor
common_mistakes.fix: Never call glUseProgram() on a program whose GL_LINK_STATUS is GL_FALSE. Validate before activating and fall back to a default error shader if linking fails.

common_mistakes.trap: Using a uniform name in glGetUniformLocation() that does not exactly match the GLSL declaration
common_mistakes.symptom: glGetUniformLocation returns -1 — uniform uploads silently do nothing, shader uses default zero values
common_mistakes.fix: Uniform names are case-sensitive and must match the GLSL declaration exactly, including array notation (e.g. uLights[0] not uLights). Check for -1 on every cached location and log a warning.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to delete shader objects after linking
common_mistakes.symptom: GPU memory leak — shader source objects accumulate on the GPU over multiple shader reloads or scene changes
common_mistakes.fix: Call glDetachShader() then glDeleteShader() for every compiled shader object immediately after glLinkProgram() succeeds. The program retains the compiled binary — the source object is no longer needed.

common_mistakes.trap: Writing #version directive that does not match the actual GL context version
common_mistakes.symptom: Shader compilation fails with 'version directive must occur before anything else' or 'unsupported version'
common_mistakes.fix: The #version directive must be the very first line of every GLSL file with no preceding whitespace or comments. For OpenGL 3.3 core use #version 330 core. Verify the actual GLSL version from glGetString(GL_SHADING_LANGUAGE_VERSION).

common_mistakes.trap: Uploading uniforms before calling glUseProgram()
common_mistakes.symptom: Uniforms have no effect — values are uploaded to whatever program was last active, not the intended one
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glUseProgram(programID) before any glUniform* call. Uniform uploads target the currently active program, not the one whose location was queried.

Define index data as a Java int array — each triplet references 3 vertex positions forming one triangle

Generate the VBO handle — generates the ID only, no GPU memory allocated yet
Bind VBO to GL_ARRAY_BUFFER slot — all subsequent vertex data uploads target this buffer
Wrap Java float array in a direct NIO FloatBuffer — OpenGL cannot read Java heap arrays directly
Upload vertex data from CPU buffer to GPU — allocates GPU memory and copies data in one call
Generate the EBO handle — separate buffer object for index data
Bind EBO to GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER — this binding is stored inside the active VAO
Wrap index array in a direct NIO IntBuffer — same requirement as vertex data
Upload index data to GPU — EBO must be uploaded while VAO is still bound
Describe attribute 0 (position) layout — tells GPU how to read xyz floats from the VBO
Enable attribute slot 0 — disabled by default, must enable each attribute after describing it
Describe attribute 1 (UV coords) layout — offset 12 bytes past the xyz position data
Enable attribute slot 1 — each attribute must be individually enabled after its pointer is set
Describe attribute 2 (normals) layout — offset 20 bytes past position and UV data
Enable attribute slot 2 — complete the normal attribute setup
Unbind the VAO — seals all recorded state, prevents accidental modification by later buffer operations
Unbind the VBO — clean state after setup, safe since VAO already recorded the binding

code_example.location: Mesh.java → standalone class, called from Engine.java → init()
code_example.description: Encapsulates VAO, VBO, and EBO creation and upload. Separating mesh data into its own class allows multiple meshes to coexist cleanly, each with their own VAO.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 4 is where geometry lives on the GPU. The VAO, VBO, and EBO created here are the direct input to every draw call in the render loop. Without this phase there is nothing for the GPU to rasterize — glDrawArrays and glDrawElements both silently produce no output if no VAO is bound or if vertex attributes are not configured. The attribute layout defined here (location indices, stride, offsets) must exactly match the layout(location=N) declarations in the vertex shader created in Phase 3, making these two phases tightly coupled.
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 3 (Shader System) — the vertex shader's attribute location indices must be known before glVertexAttribPointer is called, so that attribute slots match between the VAO and the shader. Phase 2 (GL Context Initialization) — GL.createCapabilities() must have run before glGenVertexArrays or glGenBuffers are available.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 8 (Render Loop) — glBindVertexArray(vao) and glDrawElements are called every frame inside the loop. The VAO must be fully configured before the loop starts. Phase 5 (Texture Loading) — textures are independent of mesh setup but are typically loaded in the same init block immediately after mesh setup, before the render loop begins. Phase 11 (Cleanup) — glDeleteVertexArrays and glDeleteBuffers must be called at shutdown to free the GPU memory allocated here.

common_mistakes.trap: Unbinding the EBO while the VAO is still bound
common_mistakes.symptom: glDrawElements produces no output or draws garbage — the VAO records the EBO unbind and loses its index buffer reference
common_mistakes.fix: Never call glBindBuffer(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, 0) while the VAO is bound. The EBO binding is stored inside the VAO. Only unbind the EBO after glBindVertexArray(0) has been called.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to call flip() on the NIO buffer before passing it to glBufferData()
common_mistakes.symptom: Black screen or garbage geometry — the buffer position is at the end after put(), so GL reads zero bytes or uninitialized memory
common_mistakes.fix: Always call .flip() after .put() on any NIO buffer before passing it to glBufferData() or glBufferSubData(). flip() sets limit=position and position=0, making the data readable by OpenGL.

common_mistakes.trap: Configuring glVertexAttribPointer() before binding the VBO
common_mistakes.symptom: VAO records no data source — draw calls read from buffer 0 (default) producing garbage or invisible geometry
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, vbo) before glVertexAttribPointer(). The attrib pointer records which VBO is currently bound to GL_ARRAY_BUFFER at the time of the call.

common_mistakes.trap: Wrong stride value in glVertexAttribPointer()
common_mistakes.symptom: Geometry is scrambled — vertices are offset incorrectly and positions bleed into normals or UVs
common_mistakes.fix: Stride is the total byte size of one complete vertex including all attributes. For a layout of vec3+vec2+vec3, stride = (3+2+3) * Float.BYTES = 32. If all attributes are tightly packed with no interleaving, stride can be 0.

common_mistakes.trap: Wrong pointer offset in glVertexAttribPointer() for interleaved attributes
common_mistakes.symptom: UVs or normals appear scrambled — the GPU reads the right stride but starts at the wrong byte within each vertex
common_mistakes.fix: The pointer parameter is the byte offset from the start of one vertex to this attribute. For a vec3 position followed by vec2 UV: position offset=0L, UV offset=3L*Float.BYTES, normals offset=5L*Float.BYTES.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to call glEnableVertexAttribArray() for each attribute
common_mistakes.symptom: Attributes read as zero — positions may work if location 0 happens to be enabled by default, but UVs and normals are all zero
common_mistakes.fix: Every attribute slot used in the vertex shader must be explicitly enabled with glEnableVertexAttribArray(index) after its glVertexAttribPointer() call. Disabled attributes always return zero to the shader.

common_mistakes.trap: Binding a new VAO while a previous VAO setup is incomplete
common_mistakes.symptom: Second mesh draws correctly but first mesh is broken — VAO state was captured mid-configuration
common_mistakes.fix: Always fully complete one VAO setup (all attrib pointers, all enables, EBO upload) before calling glBindVertexArray() for a new VAO. Keep each Mesh object's setup self-contained in its own constructor.

common_mistakes.trap: Using a Java heap FloatBuffer instead of a direct NIO buffer
common_mistakes.symptom: LWJGL throws IllegalArgumentException: buffer is not direct — glBufferData refuses non-direct buffers
common_mistakes.fix: Always use BufferUtils.createFloatBuffer(n) or MemoryUtil.memAllocFloat(n) to create direct off-heap buffers. Standard Java float[] arrays and ByteBuffer.allocate() produce heap buffers that OpenGL cannot read.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glDrawElements() with the wrong index count
common_mistakes.symptom: Only part of the mesh renders, or GL_INVALID_VALUE error — count does not match the actual number of indices in the EBO
common_mistakes.fix: The count parameter in glDrawElements() must equal indices.length — the total number of individual index values, not the number of triangles. Store indexCount = indices.length at mesh creation and reuse it in every draw call.

parameters.description: The pixel buffer to free. Do not use the buffer after this call.

glTexImage2D(GL_TEXTURE_2D, 0, GL_RGBA, w.get(0), h.get(0), 0, GL_RGBA, GL_UNSIGNED_BYTE, pixels);

code_example.location: Texture.java → standalone class, called from Engine.java → init()
code_example.description: Encapsulates the full STB image load → GPU upload → parameter setup → free cycle. One Texture instance per image file. Bound per draw call in the render loop.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 5 provides the surface detail that shaders sample during rendering. Without textures bound to the correct texture units, sampler2D uniforms in the fragment shader read from an empty unit and return black or undefined color. Textures are the primary link between CPU-side image files and the fragment shader — the pixel data loaded from disk here becomes the input to every texture() call in GLSL. This phase also introduces the concept of texture units (GL_TEXTURE0, GL_TEXTURE1), which must stay consistent between glActiveTexture, glBindTexture, and the glUniform1i sampler assignment in Phase 7.
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 4 (Mesh Setup) — mesh geometry must exist before textures are useful, as UV coordinates stored in the VBO are what drive texture sampling coordinates in the fragment shader. Phase 3 (Shader System) — the fragment shader must be linked before sampler uniform locations can be queried and assigned texture unit indices. Phase 2 (GL Context Initialization) — GL.createCapabilities() must have run before glGenTextures or glTexImage2D are available.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 7 (Uniform Management) — glUniform1i must assign the correct texture unit index to each sampler2D uniform after the texture is created and before any draw call uses it. Phase 8 (Render Loop) — glActiveTexture and glBindTexture must be called every frame before each draw call that requires a texture. Phase 11 (Cleanup) — glDeleteTextures must be called at shutdown to free all GPU texture memory allocated here.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to call stbi_set_flip_vertically_on_load(true) before stbi_load()
common_mistakes.symptom: Texture appears vertically flipped on all geometry — the image is upside down relative to UV coordinates
common_mistakes.fix: Call STBImage.stbi_set_flip_vertically_on_load(true) once before any stbi_load() call. OpenGL's UV origin is bottom-left but image files store pixels top-to-bottom. This call is global — set it once during init.

common_mistakes.trap: Not checking if stbi_load() returned null before calling glTexImage2D()
common_mistakes.symptom: JVM crash or GL_INVALID_OPERATION — null ByteBuffer passed to glTexImage2D causes undefined behaviour
common_mistakes.fix: Always null-check the ByteBuffer returned by stbi_load(). If null, call STBImage.stbi_failure_reason() for the error message and abort. Common causes: wrong file path, unsupported format, or corrupt file.

common_mistakes.trap: Not calling stbi_image_free() after glTexImage2D()
common_mistakes.symptom: Native memory leak — each loaded texture permanently consumes off-heap memory that the JVM GC cannot reclaim
common_mistakes.fix: Call STBImage.stbi_image_free(pixels) immediately after glTexImage2D() uploads the data. The GPU has its own copy — the CPU-side buffer is no longer needed and must be freed manually.

common_mistakes.trap: Setting GL_TEXTURE_MIN_FILTER to GL_LINEAR_MIPMAP_LINEAR without calling glGenerateMipmap()
common_mistakes.symptom: Texture renders as solid black — the GPU tries to sample mipmaps that do not exist
common_mistakes.fix: Either call glGenerateMipmap(GL_TEXTURE_2D) after glTexImage2D(), or change the min filter to GL_LINEAR which does not require mipmaps. GL_LINEAR_MIPMAP_LINEAR requires a complete mipmap chain.

common_mistakes.trap: Binding a texture to the wrong texture unit or not calling glActiveTexture() before glBindTexture()
common_mistakes.symptom: Shader samples from an empty unit and returns black — the texture is bound to unit 0 but the sampler uniform points to unit 1, or vice versa
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glActiveTexture(GL_TEXTURE0 + N) before glBindTexture(). Then set the sampler uniform to N with glUniform1i(location, N). The unit index in glActiveTexture and the integer in glUniform1i must match.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glTexImage2D() with width or height of 0
common_mistakes.symptom: GL_INVALID_VALUE error — texture is created but has no storage, sampling returns black
common_mistakes.fix: Verify w.get(0) and h.get(0) are both greater than 0 after stbi_load(). A successful load always produces positive dimensions. If either is 0 the file was not loaded correctly.

common_mistakes.trap: Setting texture parameters after glTexImage2D() instead of before
common_mistakes.symptom: Parameters appear to have no effect on some drivers — filtering or wrapping behaves unexpectedly
common_mistakes.fix: Call all glTexParameteri() calls before glTexImage2D(). While the GL spec allows parameters to be set at any time, some drivers apply them at upload time. Set wrap and filter modes immediately after glBindTexture() and before data upload.

common_mistakes.trap: Using GL_REPEAT wrap mode on an FBO color attachment texture
common_mistakes.symptom: Post-process shader shows edge-bleeding artifacts — pixels from one edge of the screen wrap around and appear on the opposite edge
common_mistakes.fix: Always use GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE for FBO color attachment textures. GL_REPEAT is correct for tiling world textures but causes visible seam artifacts when a post-process shader samples near the framebuffer borders.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to re-bind the texture before each draw call in the render loop
common_mistakes.symptom: Some objects render with the wrong texture or no texture — texture binding is overwritten by a previous draw call
common_mistakes.fix: Bind the correct texture to its unit immediately before each draw call. Texture bindings are global GL state — any intervening glBindTexture() call changes what all subsequent draw calls sample from.

code_example.location: Camera.java → standalone class, called from Engine.java → init() and renderLoop()
code_example.description: Encapsulates all camera state and matrix construction. The render loop calls update() for input and getViewMatrix() to retrieve the rebuilt view matrix each frame.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 6 is the mathematical core of the pipeline — it determines where objects appear on screen and how the world is viewed. The three matrices produced here (model, view, projection) form the MVP transform that every vertex passes through in the vertex shader. Without correct matrices uploaded to the shader, geometry either does not appear, appears in the wrong position, or is not projected correctly into screen space. This phase bridges the gap between the abstract 3D world (object positions, camera location) and the 2D pixels the GPU ultimately produces. It runs partially in init() (projection matrix, pre-allocation) and partially every frame in renderLoop() (view and model rebuild).
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 3 (Shader System) — the vertex shader must declare and use uModel, uView, and uProjection uniforms, and the program must be linked before matrix uniform locations can be queried. Phase 5 (Texture Loading) — textures and matrices are independent but both are set up in init() before the render loop. Phase 2 (GL Context Initialization) — JOML matrix operations are pure Java but the upload calls (glUniformMatrix4fv) require GL capabilities to be loaded.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 7 (Uniform Management) — matrix upload via glUniformMatrix4fv is the primary responsibility of Phase 7. The matrices built here are the values Phase 7 delivers to the shader each frame. Phase 8 (Render Loop) — the view matrix must be rebuilt from camera state every frame before uniforms are uploaded. The projection matrix must be rebuilt whenever the window is resized. Phase 9 (Depth Testing) — correct depth sorting of transparent objects requires camera position, which is owned by this phase.

common_mistakes.trap: Applying model transforms in the wrong TRS order
common_mistakes.symptom: Objects appear in wrong positions, scale distorts rotation, or rotation pivots around the wrong point
common_mistakes.fix: Always apply transforms in Translate → Rotate → Scale order: model.identity().translate().rotate().scale(). Reversing the order produces different results because matrix multiplication is not commutative. Scale last prevents rotation skew.

common_mistakes.trap: Not calling matrix.identity() before rebuilding a matrix each frame
common_mistakes.symptom: Transforms accumulate across frames — objects accelerate, spin faster each frame, or fly off screen
common_mistakes.fix: Call model.identity() and view.identity() at the start of each matrix rebuild. JOML mutates matrices in place — without resetting to identity, each frame's transforms stack on top of the previous frame's values.

common_mistakes.trap: Passing degrees instead of radians to JOML rotate() or perspective()
common_mistakes.symptom: Objects rotate at wildly incorrect angles or the scene is entirely black — FOV of 45 degrees passed as 45.0f radians produces an extremely narrow or inverted frustum
common_mistakes.fix: JOML expects radians everywhere. Always convert: (float)Math.toRadians(degrees). For perspective(), a typical 45° FOV must be passed as (float)Math.toRadians(45.0). Never pass raw degree values.

common_mistakes.trap: Allocating new Matrix4f() or new Vector3f() every frame inside the render loop
common_mistakes.symptom: Excessive GC pressure — frame rate stutters periodically as the JVM pauses to collect thousands of short-lived matrix objects
common_mistakes.fix: Pre-allocate all matrices and vectors as fields before the render loop and reuse them every frame. Call identity() to reset rather than constructing a new object. Only allocate temporary vectors when absolutely necessary.

common_mistakes.trap: Not normalizing the camera front vector after rebuilding from yaw and pitch
common_mistakes.symptom: Camera movement speed varies with look direction — faster when looking diagonally, slower on cardinal axes. lookAt produces a skewed view matrix
common_mistakes.fix: Always call cameraFront.normalize() after computing front from yaw and pitch angles. The trigonometric formula does not guarantee a unit-length vector due to floating point precision.

common_mistakes.trap: Building the view matrix before updating camera position from input
common_mistakes.symptom: Camera lags one frame behind input — movement feels delayed and unresponsive
common_mistakes.fix: Always process input and update cameraPos first, then call view.lookAt(). The view matrix must reflect the camera state after input for that frame, not before.

common_mistakes.trap: Not rebuilding the projection matrix when the window is resized
common_mistakes.symptom: Scene stretches or squashes after resize — aspect ratio is wrong because projection was built with original window dimensions
common_mistakes.fix: Recalculate the projection matrix inside the framebuffer size callback: projection.perspective(fov, (float)newWidth/newHeight, near, far). Aspect ratio must always match the current viewport dimensions.

common_mistakes.trap: Passing transpose=true to glUniformMatrix4fv with a JOML matrix
common_mistakes.symptom: Scene renders with completely wrong geometry — objects appear mirrored, inverted, or at incorrect positions
common_mistakes.fix: JOML stores matrices in column-major order, which matches OpenGL's expected layout. Always pass false for the transpose parameter in glUniformMatrix4fv. Passing true transposes the matrix unnecessarily and corrupts the transform.

common_mistakes.trap: Using the same FloatBuffer for multiple matrix uploads without calling matrix.get(buffer) in between
common_mistakes.symptom: All matrix uniforms receive the same value — the last matrix written overwrites the buffer before earlier uploads are consumed
common_mistakes.fix: Call matrix.get(buffer) immediately before each glUniformMatrix4fv() call. JOML's get() rewinds the buffer automatically, making it safe to reuse the same FloatBuffer for sequential uploads.

execution_order.reason: Uniforms only exist after linking — never query locations before glLinkProgram completes successfully
Activate the program before querying or setting any uniforms — locations are program-specific
Cache the model matrix uniform location once after linking — glGetUniformLocation is slow, never call inside the render loop
Cache view matrix location — store all locations in fields for reuse every frame
Cache projection matrix location — query all uniform locations together immediately after linking
Cache sampler uniform location — sampler2D uniforms use glUniform1i to assign a texture unit index
Cache any additional uniform locations — light position, color, time, etc. all queried here once

execution_order.call: if (modelLoc == -1) System.err.println("WARNING: uModel uniform not found")
execution_order.reason: Check for -1 on every cached location — -1 means the uniform is missing or was optimised away by the driver
Assign texture unit 0 to the sampler uniform once after linking — only needs reassigning if the program changes
All steps below execute once per frame — use cached locations only, never call glGetUniformLocation here
Activate the correct shader program at the start of each frame — uniforms must be set on the active program
Upload model matrix — call after model matrix is fully built (translate → rotate → scale complete)
Upload view matrix — call after camera position and direction are updated for this frame
Upload projection matrix — only strictly necessary each frame if aspect ratio can change; otherwise upload once
Upload light position — update per frame if the light moves, otherwise upload once after program activation
Upload elapsed time — always per-frame for animated shaders
Bind textures before draw call — the sampler uniform already points to unit 0 from step 9
Issue draw call only after all uniforms are uploaded and textures are bound — order is critical

code_example.location: UniformManager.java → standalone class, used by ShaderProgram.java and Engine.java → renderLoop()
code_example.description: Centralises all uniform location caching and upload calls. Keeps raw glUniform* calls out of the render loop and makes adding new uniforms a one-line change.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 7 is the data delivery layer between the CPU and the GPU shader. Every value the shader needs that is not vertex data — matrices, light positions, colors, time, texture unit indices — arrives through the uniform system built here. Without uniforms, shaders can only operate on hardcoded values written directly in GLSL. This phase is the communication channel that makes shaders dynamic and reusable across different objects, lights, and frames. It sits at the intersection of every other phase — it receives matrices from Phase 6, texture unit assignments from Phase 5, and delivers everything to the shader program from Phase 3, all timed within the render loop of Phase 8.
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 3 (Shader System) — glGetUniformLocation requires a successfully linked program. All uniform locations must be queried and cached immediately after glLinkProgram and before the render loop starts. Phase 6 (Camera & Transformation Matrices) — matrices must be built before they can be uploaded. Phase 5 (Texture Loading) — sampler2D uniforms must be assigned their texture unit index (glUniform1i) after the program is linked and before the first draw call.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 8 (Render Loop) — all per-frame uniform uploads (matrices, light data, time) happen at the start of each frame inside the loop, before any draw call is issued. glDrawElements and glDrawArrays read whatever uniform values are currently set — uploading after the draw call has no effect on that frame. Phase 9 (Depth Testing) — transparent pass rendering may require additional uniforms such as opacity or blend weight that must be uploaded before each transparent draw call.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glGetUniformLocation() every frame inside the render loop
common_mistakes.symptom: Severe and consistent frame rate drop — CPU stalls waiting for driver response on every frame for every uniform
common_mistakes.fix: Query all uniform locations once after glLinkProgram() and cache them in fields or a HashMap. Only use the cached integer locations inside the render loop. glGetUniformLocation is a synchronous driver call that blocks the CPU.

common_mistakes.trap: Uploading uniforms before calling glUseProgram()
common_mistakes.symptom: Uniforms have no effect — values are silently uploaded to whichever program was last active, not the intended one
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glUseProgram(programID) before any glUniform* call. Uniform state is per-program — uploading without an active program either targets the wrong program or generates GL_INVALID_OPERATION.

common_mistakes.trap: Not checking for -1 returned by glGetUniformLocation()
common_mistakes.symptom: Uniform uploads silently do nothing — shader uses default zero values, scene renders incorrectly with no error
common_mistakes.fix: Check every cached location against -1 after querying. A return of -1 means the uniform name was not found, is misspelled, or was optimised away by the driver because it is unused in the shader. Log a warning for every -1.

common_mistakes.trap: Passing transpose=true to glUniformMatrix4fv() with JOML matrices
common_mistakes.symptom: All transformed geometry appears at wrong positions or is completely invisible — matrix data is interpreted in row-major order instead of column-major
common_mistakes.fix: Always pass false for transpose. JOML matrices are column-major which matches OpenGL's expected layout exactly. Transposing corrupts every transform in the scene.

common_mistakes.trap: Assigning the wrong integer to a sampler2D uniform with glUniform1i()
common_mistakes.symptom: Shader samples from the wrong texture unit — objects show incorrect textures or solid black if the assigned unit has nothing bound
common_mistakes.fix: The integer passed to glUniform1i(samplerLoc, N) must equal the texture unit index N used in glActiveTexture(GL_TEXTURE0 + N). If you bind a texture to GL_TEXTURE1, assign 1 to the sampler uniform, not 0.

common_mistakes.trap: Uploading a mat4 uniform with glUniform4fv() instead of glUniformMatrix4fv()
common_mistakes.symptom: Geometry renders at completely wrong positions — the 16 floats are interpreted as 4 vec4s instead of one column-major matrix
common_mistakes.fix: Use glUniformMatrix4fv() for mat4 uniforms, glUniform3fv() for vec3 arrays, and glUniform1fv() for float arrays. The function name must match the GLSL type exactly — using the wrong variant silently corrupts the upload.

common_mistakes.trap: Not re-uploading per-object uniforms between draw calls for multiple objects
common_mistakes.symptom: All objects render at the same position — every object uses the model matrix from the last glUniformMatrix4fv() call
common_mistakes.fix: Upload uModel before every individual draw call in the object loop. Uniform state is global — the last uploaded value applies to all subsequent draw calls until changed.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to set sampler uniforms after switching shader programs
common_mistakes.symptom: Post-process or secondary shader samples from unit 0 by default but the texture is bound to a different unit — black output
common_mistakes.fix: Sampler uniform assignments (glUniform1i) are stored per-program. After linking any new shader program, call glUseProgram() and reassign all sampler uniforms to their correct texture unit indices. They do not carry over from other programs.

common_mistakes.trap: Using a mat3 normal matrix location but uploading with glUniformMatrix4fv() instead of glUniformMatrix3fv()
common_mistakes.symptom: Lighting calculations are incorrect — normals are transformed by the wrong matrix size and produce garbage lighting vectors
common_mistakes.fix: A mat3 uniform requires glUniformMatrix3fv() with a FloatBuffer of exactly 9 floats. Use Matrix3f from JOML and BufferUtils.createFloatBuffer(9) for the upload buffer, not the 16-float Matrix4f buffer.

Rebuild view matrix from updated camera state — must reflect input changes processed in step 5
Upload view matrix to shader — must be uploaded after it is rebuilt and before the draw call
Upload projection matrix — upload every frame for safety, or only on window resize if performance critical

Upload this object's model matrix — must be uploaded before the draw call for each individual object

Swap front and back buffers — presents the completed frame to the screen; call after ALL draw calls are done
Return to the while condition — continue until glfwWindowShouldClose returns true

code_example.location: Engine.java → renderLoop() method, called from main() after init()
code_example.description: The complete per-frame sequence. Shows the exact order of delta time → input → logic → clear → bind → uniforms → draw → swap. All phases converge here.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 8 is the heartbeat of the engine — every other phase either feeds into it or is driven by it. All setup phases (1–7) exist solely to prepare resources that the render loop consumes every frame. The loop itself is the only place where actual rendering happens: it orchestrates the exact per-frame sequence of input → logic → clear → bind → uniforms → draw → swap that produces visible output. Nothing is drawn to the screen outside of this loop. Frame timing (deltaTime) calculated here governs camera movement from Phase 6, animation updates, and any time-based uniform from Phase 7. The loop also enforces the opaque-before-transparent draw order required by Phase 9.
integration_context.comes_after: All setup phases must be complete before the loop starts. Phase 1 (Window) — the window handle and event system must exist for glfwWindowShouldClose and glfwPollEvents. Phase 2 (GL Init) — GL state must be configured. Phase 3 (Shader) — shader program must be linked and active. Phase 4 (Mesh) — VAO must be configured and ready to bind. Phase 5 (Texture) — textures must be uploaded and parameters set. Phase 6 (Camera) — projection matrix must be pre-built and camera state initialised. Phase 7 (Uniforms) — all uniform locations must be cached and sampler assignments made.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 9 (Depth Testing & Blending) — the opaque and transparent sub-passes described in Phase 9 are sub-sections of this loop, executed in strict order within each frame. Phase 10 (Framebuffer) — the two-pass FBO pattern wraps the entire render loop body: the scene draw is redirected into the FBO, then a screen quad pass follows before glfwSwapBuffers. Phase 11 (Cleanup) — the loop exits when glfwWindowShouldClose returns true, at which point cleanup() is called immediately in main().

common_mistakes.trap: Not scaling movement and animation by deltaTime
common_mistakes.symptom: Camera and object movement speed is tied to frame rate — runs fast on high-end GPUs, slow on low-end hardware
common_mistakes.fix: Multiply every per-frame velocity, rotation speed, and animation step by deltaTime. deltaTime = currentTime - lastFrameTime ensures consistent behaviour regardless of how fast or slow the loop runs.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glfwSwapBuffers() before all draw calls are complete
common_mistakes.symptom: Partial frame appears on screen — some objects from the current frame and some from the previous frame are visible simultaneously
common_mistakes.fix: glfwSwapBuffers() must be the very last call of the frame, after every draw call, every uniform upload, and every state change. Never swap mid-frame.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to call glfwPollEvents() inside the render loop
common_mistakes.symptom: Window becomes unresponsive — OS marks it as not responding, keyboard and mouse input never fires, window cannot be closed
common_mistakes.fix: Call glfwPollEvents() every frame inside the render loop. It processes all pending OS events and updates input state. Without it, the window event queue fills up and the application appears frozen to the OS.

common_mistakes.trap: Clearing only GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT without GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT
common_mistakes.symptom: Depth buffer accumulates values from previous frames — geometry from earlier frames bleeds through current geometry, z-fighting artifacts on every frame
common_mistakes.fix: Always clear both: glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT). If stencil is in use add GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT. Never clear color alone when depth testing is active.

common_mistakes.trap: Drawing transparent objects before opaque objects
common_mistakes.symptom: Transparent geometry looks correct in isolation but opaque geometry behind it is invisible or partially missing
common_mistakes.fix: Always draw all opaque geometry first with depth writes enabled, then draw transparent geometry sorted back-to-front with depth writes disabled. Transparent fragments need the opaque depth buffer to test against.

common_mistakes.trap: Not sorting transparent objects back-to-front before drawing
common_mistakes.symptom: Transparent objects show incorrect blending — objects behind appear in front, blending order is wrong depending on camera angle
common_mistakes.fix: Sort transparent objects by distance from camera (farthest first) every frame before the transparent pass. Blending is order-dependent — GL_SRC_ALPHA blending requires back-to-front order to composite correctly.

common_mistakes.trap: Leaving glDepthMask(false) set after the transparent pass
common_mistakes.symptom: Next frame's opaque geometry does not write to the depth buffer — z-fighting between all opaque objects every frame
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glDepthMask(true) immediately after the transparent pass completes, before glfwSwapBuffers(). Restore all modified GL state at the end of the pass that changed it.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glfwGetTime() multiple times per frame for deltaTime instead of storing the result
common_mistakes.symptom: deltaTime is near zero or inconsistent — the second glfwGetTime() call returns a slightly different time than the first, making deltaTime almost nothing
common_mistakes.fix: Store the result of glfwGetTime() in currentTime at the top of the loop, compute deltaTime = currentTime - lastFrameTime, then set lastFrameTime = currentTime. Use these stored values throughout the frame.

common_mistakes.trap: Binding a VAO inside the render loop without unbinding it after drawing
common_mistakes.symptom: Subsequent VAO configurations outside the loop accidentally modify the last bound VAO — mesh data is silently corrupted
common_mistakes.fix: Call glBindVertexArray(0) after each draw call or at the end of the frame. While not strictly required for correctness in a simple loop, it prevents accidental state leakage if setup code runs while a VAO is still bound.
