core_functions.description: Activates alpha blending. Required for transparent or semi-transparent objects.

parameters.name: cap
parameters.description: The capability to enable.

core_functions.name: glBlendFunc
core_functions.description: Defines how source (incoming fragment) and destination (framebuffer) colors are combined during blending.

parameters.name: sfactor
parameters.description: Multiplier applied to the incoming fragment color.

alternatives.use_when: Standard transparency — scales source by its own alpha
Additive blending source factor — light/fire/glow effects
Pre-multiplied alpha source factor

parameters.name: dfactor
parameters.description: Multiplier applied to the existing framebuffer color.

alternatives.use_when: Standard transparency — scales destination by (1 - source alpha)
Additive blending destination factor — adds to existing color
Replace destination completely with source

core_functions.name: glEnable(GL_CULL_FACE)
core_functions.description: Activates face culling. Discards back-facing triangles before rasterization, improving performance.

parameters.name: cap
parameters.description: The capability to enable.

core_functions.name: glCullFace
core_functions.description: Specifies which face(s) are culled when GL_CULL_FACE is enabled.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: Which faces to discard.

alternatives.use_when: Cull back faces — standard for closed 3D meshes (default)
Cull front faces — used in shadow volume or inside-out rendering techniques
Cull all faces — only points and lines render; useful for wireframe debug

core_functions.name: glFrontFace
core_functions.description: Defines which winding order of vertices is considered the front face.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: The vertex winding order that defines a front-facing triangle.

alternatives.use_when: Counter-clockwise winding = front face — OpenGL default, used by most exporters
Clockwise winding = front face — use if mesh normals appear inverted

supporting_functions.name: glBlendFuncSeparate
supporting_functions.signature: void glBlendFuncSeparate(int sfactorRGB, int dfactorRGB, int sfactorAlpha, int dfactorAlpha)
supporting_functions.description: Sets independent blend factors for the RGB and alpha channels. Use when rendering to an FBO that will itself be composited — prevents double-multiplying the alpha channel.

parameters.name: sfactorRGB
parameters.description: Source blend factor applied to the RGB channels of the incoming fragment.

alternatives.use_when: Standard transparency source factor for RGB
Additive blending — light/glow effects

parameters.name: dfactorRGB
parameters.description: Destination blend factor applied to the RGB channels in the framebuffer.

alternatives.use_when: Standard transparency destination factor for RGB
Additive destination — accumulates light

parameters.name: sfactorAlpha
parameters.description: Source blend factor applied only to the alpha channel.

alternatives.use_when: Preserve full source alpha when compositing FBOs
Scale source alpha by itself — premultiplied alpha workflows

parameters.name: dfactorAlpha
parameters.description: Destination blend factor applied only to the alpha channel.

alternatives.use_when: Standard alpha compositing for FBO output
Replace destination alpha entirely with source alpha

supporting_functions.example_call: glBlendFuncSeparate(GL_SRC_ALPHA, GL_ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA, GL_ONE, GL_ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA);

supporting_functions.name: glBlendEquation
supporting_functions.description: Controls how the source and destination blend results are combined. Default is addition — override for subtractive or min/max blending effects.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: The equation used to combine blended source and destination values.

alternatives.use_when: src + dst — default, standard transparency and additive blending
src - dst — subtractive effects, darkening
dst - src — inverted subtractive
min(src, dst) — darkening compositing, shadow overlay
max(src, dst) — brightening compositing, HDR highlight accumulation

supporting_functions.example_call:
glBlendEquation(GL_FUNC_ADD); // default — restore after special effects
glBlendEquation(GL_MIN);      // darkening pass

supporting_functions.name: glColorMask
supporting_functions.signature: void glColorMask(boolean red, boolean green, boolean blue, boolean alpha)
supporting_functions.description: Controls which color channels are written during rendering. Disable all channels to render only to the depth buffer — used in shadow map first passes.

parameters.name: red
parameters.description: Whether the red channel is written to the framebuffer.

parameters.name: green
parameters.description: Whether the green channel is written to the framebuffer.

parameters.name: blue
parameters.description: Whether the blue channel is written to the framebuffer.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description: Whether the alpha channel is written to the framebuffer.

supporting_functions.name: glStencilFunc
supporting_functions.signature: void glStencilFunc(int func, int ref, int mask)
supporting_functions.description: Sets the stencil test comparison function. Controls which fragments pass or fail the stencil test based on a reference value. Requires glEnable(GL_STENCIL_TEST).

parameters.name: func
parameters.description: The comparison function for the stencil test.

alternatives.use_when: Always pass — use when writing to the stencil buffer on first pass
Pass if stencil value equals ref — use on second pass to draw only masked areas
Pass if stencil value does not equal ref — draw outside masked area (outline effect)
Never pass — nothing renders, useful for stencil-only write passes

parameters.name: ref
parameters.description: The reference value compared against the stencil buffer value.

parameters.name: mask
parameters.description: Bitmask ANDed with both ref and the stencil value before comparison. Use 0xFF to test all bits.

supporting_functions.name: glStencilOp
supporting_functions.signature: void glStencilOp(int sfail, int dpfail, int dppass)
supporting_functions.description: Defines what happens to the stencil buffer value under each test outcome. Controls whether the buffer is written, incremented, or cleared.

parameters.name: sfail
parameters.description: Action when the stencil test fails.

alternatives.use_when: Leave stencil value unchanged — default for all three params
Write the ref value from glStencilFunc — use to stamp a mask
Clear stencil to 0
Increment stencil value — shadow volume counting
Decrement stencil value — shadow volume counting

parameters.name: dpfail
parameters.description: Action when the stencil test passes but the depth test fails.

parameters.name: dppass
parameters.description: Action when both stencil and depth tests pass.

supporting_functions.example_call: glStencilOp(GL_KEEP, GL_KEEP, GL_REPLACE); // write ref on depth+stencil pass

supporting_functions.name: glStencilMask
supporting_functions.description: Controls which stencil buffer bits can be written. Use 0xFF to enable writes, 0x00 to make the stencil buffer read-only during a rendering pass.

parameters.name: mask
parameters.description: Bit mask for stencil writes. Only bits set to 1 can be modified.

alternatives.use_when: Enable all stencil bit writes — use during the stencil write pass
Disable all stencil writes — read-only mode during the render pass

supporting_functions.name: glClearStencil
supporting_functions.description: Sets the value used to clear the stencil buffer when GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT is passed to glClear. Default is 0.

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: The value to fill the stencil buffer with on clear.

supporting_functions.example_call:
glClearStencil(0);
glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT | GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT);

execution_order.reason: All default GL state is configured once during engine initialisation — before the render loop
Activate depth testing globally — keeps correct 3D occlusion for all opaque geometry
Set default depth comparison — fragments pass only if closer than what is already in the depth buffer
Activate back-face culling globally — skip rendering hidden triangle faces for performance
Target back faces for culling — matches standard counter-clockwise front-face convention
Declare counter-clockwise winding as front-facing — must match vertex winding in all mesh data
Opaque objects are rendered first with full depth writes enabled — establishes correct depth buffer
Clear all buffers at the start of each frame — depth must be cleared so previous frame values do not persist
Ensure depth writes are ON for the opaque pass — may have been turned off in a previous transparent pass
Disable blending for opaque geometry — blending is not needed and wastes GPU time on opaque surfaces
Draw all opaque geometry first — depth buffer is fully populated before any transparent pass begins
Transparent objects must be drawn after all opaque geometry, sorted back-to-front by distance from camera
Sort transparent objects from farthest to nearest — blending is order-dependent; wrong order causes visual artifacts
Activate blending for the transparent pass — must be enabled before drawing any semi-transparent geometry
Set standard alpha transparency blend equation — source alpha controls opacity, destination is scaled inversely
Disable depth writes for transparent geometry — transparent objects test depth but must not overwrite it, preserving opaque occlusion

execution_order.call: glDrawElements(GL_TRIANGLES, indexCount, GL_UNSIGNED_INT, 0L)  // transparent objects, back-to-front
execution_order.reason: Draw transparent geometry in sorted order — blending combines each fragment with what is already in the framebuffer
Restore depth writes after transparent pass — must be re-enabled before the next frame's opaque pass
Disable blending after transparent pass — leave GL state clean for the next frame's opaque pass
Stencil effects run as a separate pass after opaque and transparent geometry is drawn
Activate stencil testing for the effect pass — only enabled when a stencil technique is in use
Clear stencil buffer and enable writes before the write pass — ensures no stale stencil values from prior frames
Write pass — always write the reference value 1 into stencil wherever geometry passes depth test

execution_order.call: glDrawElements(...)  // draw object to stamp stencil mask
execution_order.reason: Render the object normally — stamps a 1 into the stencil buffer wherever the object's pixels land
Read pass — only pass fragments where stencil != 1 (outside the object), lock stencil writes

execution_order.call: glDrawElements(...)  // draw slightly scaled object with outline shader
execution_order.reason: Draw a slightly enlarged version with a flat outline color — only pixels outside the original mask are visible
Disable stencil test and restore write mask — clean state for the next frame

code_example.location: Engine.java → init() for state setup, renderLoop() for per-frame pass switching
code_example.description: Shows the three-pass rendering pattern: opaque pass with full depth writes, transparent pass with sorted back-to-front blending, and an optional stencil outline effect. All state changes are explicit and restored after each pass.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 9 governs the correctness of how fragments are composited onto the framebuffer. Depth testing ensures that closer geometry occludes farther geometry — without it every draw call overwrites the previous one regardless of 3D position, producing visual chaos. Blending enables semi-transparent surfaces to mix with what is already in the framebuffer — without it transparency is impossible. Face culling eliminates back-facing triangles before rasterization, saving GPU work. These are not standalone features but GL state flags that must be toggled precisely around each draw pass — the wrong state at the wrong time causes z-fighting, invisible transparent objects, missing faces, or incorrectly ordered compositing. Phase 9 is woven throughout the render loop as a series of state switches between passes rather than a single block of code.
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 2 (GL Context Initialization) — glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST), glEnable(GL_CULL_FACE), and glDepthFunc are set once here during init. Phase 8 (Render Loop) — the opaque pass, transparent pass, and stencil pass are sub-sections of the render loop. glClear must wipe the depth buffer at the start of each frame before depth testing has any meaning. Phase 6 (Camera & Matrices) — correct back-to-front sorting of transparent objects requires camera position, which is owned and updated by Phase 6 each frame.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 10 (Framebuffer) — when an FBO is in use, all depth testing and blending state applies equally inside the FBO render pass. The opaque and transparent passes described here execute inside the FBO's bindForRendering() block in Phase 10, not against the screen. Phase 8 (Render Loop) — the state restoration steps (glDepthMask(true), glDisable(GL_BLEND), glDisable(GL_STENCIL_TEST)) at the end of each pass are critical to keeping the next frame's opaque pass starting from a clean, known GL state.

common_mistakes.trap: Leaving glDepthMask(false) active after the transparent pass
common_mistakes.symptom: Opaque geometry in the next frame does not write to the depth buffer — z-fighting between all objects, depth test is effectively broken
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glDepthMask(true) immediately after the transparent pass ends and before glfwSwapBuffers(). Treat every GL state change as a pair: change it before the pass, restore it after.

common_mistakes.trap: Enabling GL_BLEND globally and never disabling it between passes
common_mistakes.symptom: Opaque geometry appears semi-transparent or washed out — blending is applied to fragments that should be fully opaque
common_mistakes.fix: Disable blending with glDisable(GL_BLEND) before the opaque pass and only enable it with glEnable(GL_BLEND) for the transparent pass. Blending must be explicitly scoped to the pass that needs it.

common_mistakes.trap: Not sorting transparent objects before drawing them
common_mistakes.symptom: Transparent objects composite incorrectly — objects behind appear in front of objects that should occlude them, blending order changes as camera rotates
common_mistakes.fix: Sort all transparent objects back-to-front by distance from camera position every frame before the transparent draw pass. The correct blend result depends entirely on draw order — GL_SRC_ALPHA blending is order-dependent.

common_mistakes.trap: Using GL_LESS depth function when rendering a skybox
common_mistakes.symptom: Skybox is clipped or invisible — it fails the depth test because its geometry sits exactly at the far plane (z=1.0 in NDC)
common_mistakes.fix: Change the depth function to GL_LEQUAL before drawing the skybox: glDepthFunc(GL_LEQUAL). This allows the skybox to pass the depth test when its depth equals the far plane value. Restore GL_LESS after.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting glFrontFace(GL_CCW) when mesh winding does not match the default
common_mistakes.symptom: All geometry is invisible or inside-out — back faces are rendered and front faces are culled
common_mistakes.fix: Verify the winding order of your mesh data matches glFrontFace(). Most 3D exporters produce CCW winding. If geometry is invisible with culling enabled, temporarily disable culling with glDisable(GL_CULL_FACE) to confirm the geometry exists.

common_mistakes.trap: Not clearing the stencil buffer at the start of a frame that uses stencil effects
common_mistakes.symptom: Stencil mask from a previous frame bleeds into the current frame — outline or mask appears on objects that should not have it
common_mistakes.fix: Include GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT in glClear() whenever stencil effects are active: glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT | GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT). Also call glClearStencil(0) to define the clear value.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to restore glStencilMask(0xFF) after locking stencil writes
common_mistakes.symptom: Stencil buffer cannot be cleared in subsequent frames — glClear(GL_STENCIL_BUFFER_BIT) has no effect because the write mask is still 0x00
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glStencilMask(0xFF) after the stencil read pass to re-enable writes. The write mask applies to glClear as well as draw calls — a locked mask prevents clearing.

common_mistakes.trap: Using glBlendFunc with GL_SRC_ALPHA on an FBO that will itself be composited
common_mistakes.symptom: Alpha is double-multiplied when the FBO result is blended onto the screen — transparent areas are more transparent than intended
common_mistakes.fix: Use glBlendFuncSeparate() for FBO rendering to control RGB and alpha blending independently: glBlendFuncSeparate(GL_SRC_ALPHA, GL_ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA, GL_ONE, GL_ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA). This prevents alpha from being applied twice during compositing.

common_mistakes.trap: Enabling GL_CULL_FACE for double-sided geometry like foliage, glass, or UI quads
common_mistakes.symptom: Back faces of double-sided objects are invisible — foliage looks hollow, glass panes disappear from one side
common_mistakes.fix: Disable face culling before drawing double-sided geometry: glDisable(GL_CULL_FACE). Re-enable it after: glEnable(GL_CULL_FACE). Alternatively use two draw calls with glFrontFace toggled between GL_CCW and GL_CW.

Bind the texture so its storage can be allocated — must be configured before attaching to the FBO

Clamp vertical UV — same reason as step 7, always use CLAMP_TO_EDGE for FBO color textures

Generate a renderbuffer for depth and stencil storage — RBO is faster than a texture when depth data does not need to be sampled
Bind the RBO so storage can be allocated

Unbind the RBO after storage allocation — clean state before FBO creation
Generate the FBO handle — created after all attachments are ready
Bind the FBO so attachments can be connected to it
Attach color texture to the FBO — rendered color output writes into this texture
Attach depth+stencil RBO — FBO needs a depth buffer for correct 3D rendering inside it

Restore default screen framebuffer after setup — prevents accidentally rendering into the FBO before the loop starts
First pass each frame renders the full scene into the FBO instead of directly to screen
Redirect all rendering into the FBO — everything drawn after this goes into colorTexture and the RBO
Set viewport to match FBO dimensions — required if FBO size differs from window size
Re-confirm depth testing is active — FBO rendering uses the same GL state as screen rendering
Clear the FBO's color and depth attachments — stale data from the previous frame must be wiped
Render entire scene normally — all draw calls write into colorTexture via the FBO
Second pass reads the FBO color texture and renders a full-screen quad with a post-process shader
Restore default screen framebuffer — all subsequent draw calls target the visible window
Reset viewport to window size — necessary if FBO resolution differs from window resolution
Disable depth testing for the screen quad — a flat 2D quad does not need depth comparisons

Restore depth testing after screen quad — ensure GL state is clean for the next frame's scene pass

code_example.location: Framebuffer.java → standalone class, Engine.java → init() constructs it, renderLoop() drives the two-pass pattern
code_example.description: Encapsulates FBO creation, attachment validation, and the two-pass render pattern. Pass 1 renders the scene into the FBO. Pass 2 draws a full-screen quad through a post-process shader sampling the FBO color texture.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 10 wraps the entire render loop in a two-pass structure that enables post-processing effects. Instead of drawing directly to the screen, the full scene is first rendered into an offscreen FBO — a GPU-side render target made of a color texture and a depth renderbuffer. The result is then fed as a texture input to a second pass that draws a full-screen quad through a post-process shader, applying effects like bloom, tone mapping, gamma correction, or chromatic aberration before the final image reaches the screen. Without this phase, all rendering goes directly to the default framebuffer and no post-processing is possible. With it, the engine gains a compositing stage between scene rendering and screen output that can be extended with additional FBO passes for shadows, reflections, or deferred shading.
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 2 (GL Context Initialization) — GL.createCapabilities() must have loaded glGenFramebuffers, glFramebufferTexture2D, and all related FBO functions. Phase 3 (Shader System) — a dedicated post-process shader program must be compiled and linked separately from the scene shader, with its own sampler2D uniform pointing to the FBO color texture. Phase 4 (Mesh Setup) — the full-screen quad is itself a mesh (VAO + VBO) that must be set up during init before the render loop begins. Phase 9 (Depth Testing & Blending) — all depth and blend state for the scene pass applies inside the FBO, while the screen quad pass deliberately disables depth testing.
integration_context.comes_before: Phase 8 (Render Loop) — the FBO two-pass pattern restructures the render loop body: framebuffer.bindForRendering() replaces the raw glClear at the top of the loop, and framebuffer.drawToScreen() replaces the direct path to glfwSwapBuffers. Phase 11 (Cleanup) — glDeleteFramebuffers, glDeleteTextures (color attachment), glDeleteRenderbuffers, and the quad VAO/VBO must all be explicitly deleted during shutdown in the correct order.

common_mistakes.trap: Not calling glCheckFramebufferStatus() after assembling the FBO
common_mistakes.symptom: Rendering into the FBO produces no output or undefined behaviour — the FBO is incomplete but no error is raised unless explicitly checked
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glCheckFramebufferStatus(GL_FRAMEBUFFER) immediately after attaching all textures and renderbuffers. If the result is not GL_FRAMEBUFFER_COMPLETE, log the status code and abort. Never render into an incomplete FBO.

common_mistakes.trap: Passing a non-null ByteBuffer to glTexImage2D() when creating an FBO color attachment
common_mistakes.symptom: Texture is pre-filled with garbage CPU memory — post-process shader shows leftover data from previous allocations on the first frame
common_mistakes.fix: Pass (ByteBuffer) null as the pixel data argument to glTexImage2D() when creating an FBO attachment. This allocates empty GPU storage without uploading any CPU data — the FBO will fill it during rendering.

common_mistakes.trap: FBO color texture and depth renderbuffer have mismatched dimensions
common_mistakes.symptom: glCheckFramebufferStatus() returns GL_FRAMEBUFFER_INCOMPLETE_ATTACHMENT — FBO is invalid and refuses to render
common_mistakes.fix: The width and height passed to glTexImage2D() for the color texture and glRenderbufferStorage() for the RBO must be identical. Both must also match the glViewport() dimensions used during the FBO render pass.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to bind the FBO back to 0 after the offscreen pass
common_mistakes.symptom: The screen quad pass also renders into the FBO instead of the screen — the visible window never updates, stays black or frozen
common_mistakes.fix: Call glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, 0) to restore the default screen framebuffer before drawing the post-process quad. Every offscreen pass must be explicitly closed by restoring the default framebuffer.

common_mistakes.trap: Not disabling depth testing before drawing the full-screen quad
common_mistakes.symptom: The screen quad fails the depth test and is not rendered — screen stays black even though the FBO rendered correctly
common_mistakes.fix: Call glDisable(GL_DEPTH_TEST) before drawing the full-screen quad. The quad has no meaningful depth — it should always cover the entire screen. Re-enable glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST) after the quad is drawn.

common_mistakes.trap: Sampling the FBO color texture while it is still bound as the active render target
common_mistakes.symptom: GL_INVALID_OPERATION or undefined sampling behaviour — a texture cannot simultaneously be a read source and a write target
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, 0) to switch away from the FBO before binding its color texture for sampling in the screen pass. The FBO must be unbound before its attachment textures can be safely read.

common_mistakes.trap: Not clearing the FBO color and depth buffers at the start of the offscreen pass
common_mistakes.symptom: Previous frame's geometry persists in the FBO — ghosting effect where old frames accumulate on top of each other
common_mistakes.fix: After glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, fbo), always call glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT) before drawing the scene. The FBO is not automatically cleared between frames.

common_mistakes.trap: Using GL_REPEAT wrap mode on the FBO color attachment texture
common_mistakes.symptom: Post-process shader shows content from the opposite screen edge wrapped around — visible seam artifacts at screen borders
common_mistakes.fix: Always set GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE for both wrap modes on FBO color textures: glTexParameteri(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_S, GL_CLAMP_TO_EDGE). FBO textures represent a finite screen region and must never tile.

common_mistakes.trap: Not resizing the FBO attachments when the window is resized
common_mistakes.symptom: Post-process output is stretched, squashed, or pixelated after resize — FBO still renders at original resolution
common_mistakes.fix: When the window resizes, delete and recreate the FBO color texture and RBO at the new dimensions, then re-attach them and re-validate with glCheckFramebufferStatus(). Alternatively create the FBO at a fixed resolution and scale the quad UV coordinates.

core_functions.name: glDeleteVertexArrays
core_functions.description: Frees the GPU memory allocated for a Vertex Array Object.

parameters.name: array
parameters.type: int (user value — VAO ID from glGenVertexArrays)
parameters.description: The VAO to delete.

core_functions.name: glDeleteBuffers
core_functions.description: Frees the GPU memory allocated for a buffer object (VBO or EBO).

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.type: int (user value — buffer ID from glGenBuffers)
parameters.description: The buffer object to delete.

core_functions.name: glDeleteTextures
core_functions.description: Frees the GPU memory allocated for a texture object.

parameters.name: texture
parameters.type: int (user value — texture ID from glGenTextures)
parameters.description: The texture object to delete.

core_functions.name: glDeleteProgram
core_functions.description: Frees the GPU resources used by a linked shader program.

parameters.name: program
parameters.type: int (user value — program ID from glCreateProgram)
parameters.description: The shader program to delete.

core_functions.name: glDeleteFramebuffers
core_functions.description: Frees the GPU resources used by a Framebuffer Object. Attachments (textures, RBOs) must be deleted separately.

parameters.name: framebuffer
parameters.type: int (user value — FBO ID from glGenFramebuffers)
parameters.description: The FBO to delete.

core_functions.name: glDeleteRenderbuffers
core_functions.description: Frees the GPU memory allocated for a Renderbuffer Object.

parameters.name: renderbuffer
parameters.type: int (user value — RBO ID from glGenRenderbuffers)
parameters.description: The renderbuffer to delete.

core_functions.name: glfwDestroyWindow
core_functions.description: Destroys the GLFW window and its associated OpenGL context. Must be called before glfwTerminate.

parameters.name: window
parameters.type: long (user value — window handle from glfwCreateWindow)
parameters.description: The window to destroy.

core_functions.name: glfwTerminate
core_functions.description: Shuts down the GLFW library and frees all remaining GLFW resources. Must be the very last GLFW call.

core_functions.name: glfwSetErrorCallback(null).free()
core_functions.description: Releases the native memory held by the GLFW error callback. Call before glfwTerminate to prevent memory leaks.

supporting_functions.name: glDeleteShader
supporting_functions.description: Deletes a shader object that was not deleted immediately after linking. If already deleted post-link, this is a no-op — safe to call defensively.

parameters.name: shader
parameters.type: int (user value — shader ID from glCreateShader)
parameters.description: The shader object to delete. Pass 0 to silently no-op.

supporting_functions.example_call:
glDeleteShader(vertexShader);
glDeleteShader(fragmentShader);

supporting_functions.name: Callbacks.glfwFreeCallbacks
supporting_functions.description: Frees all GLFW callback objects registered to a window in one call. Must be called before glfwDestroyWindow to avoid native memory leaks from unreleased callback references.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: The window whose registered callbacks will all be freed.

supporting_functions.example_call:
Callbacks.glfwFreeCallbacks(window); // before glfwDestroyWindow
glfwDestroyWindow(window);

supporting_functions.name: MemoryStack.stackPush / stackPop
supporting_functions.returns: MemoryStack — the pushed stack frame (use in try-with-resources)
supporting_functions.description: LWJGL stack allocator for temporary native memory. Use instead of BufferUtils for short-lived allocations inside loops or callbacks — automatically freed on pop, no GC pressure.

supporting_functions.name: MemoryUtil.memFree
supporting_functions.description: Frees a direct NIO buffer allocated with MemoryUtil.memAlloc or MemoryUtil.memAllocFloat. If you allocated buffers with MemoryUtil instead of BufferUtils, you must free them manually here.

parameters.name: buffer
parameters.type: Buffer (user value — any direct NIO buffer allocated via MemoryUtil.memAlloc*)
parameters.description: The buffer to free. Only call on buffers allocated with MemoryUtil — not on BufferUtils buffers.

supporting_functions.name: STBImage.stbi_image_free
supporting_functions.description: Frees any STB image pixel buffers that were not freed immediately after upload. If your texture loader stores the raw buffer, free it here during shutdown.

parameters.name: image
parameters.type: ByteBuffer (user value — buffer returned by stbi_load)
parameters.description: The pixel buffer to free. Safe to call at shutdown if not freed at upload time.

supporting_functions.name: glUseProgram(0)
supporting_functions.description: Unbinds the active shader program before deletion. Best practice before glDeleteProgram — prevents driver-side use-after-free on some implementations.

parameters.name: program
parameters.description: Pass 0 to unbind any currently active shader program.

supporting_functions.example_call:
glUseProgram(0);         // unbind first
glDeleteProgram(shaderProgram);

supporting_functions.name: glBindVertexArray(0)
supporting_functions.description: Unbinds the active VAO before deletion. Best practice before glDeleteVertexArrays to ensure no VAO is active when it is freed.

parameters.name: array
parameters.description: Pass 0 to unbind the currently active VAO.

supporting_functions.example_call:
glBindVertexArray(0);       // unbind first
glDeleteVertexArrays(vao);

supporting_functions.name: glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, 0)
supporting_functions.description: Restores the default screen framebuffer before deleting any FBOs. Prevents rendering into a deleted FBO if any stale draw calls occur during shutdown.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: The framebuffer binding point to restore.

parameters.name: framebuffer
parameters.description: Pass 0 to restore the default window framebuffer.

supporting_functions.example_call:
glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, 0); // restore screen
glDeleteFramebuffers(fbo);

supporting_functions.name: System.gc
supporting_functions.description: Suggests a Java garbage collection pass after releasing all native resources. Not guaranteed to run immediately — use only as a hint at the very end of shutdown after all GL and GLFW resources are freed.
supporting_functions.example_call:
glfwTerminate();
System.gc(); // hint JVM to collect any remaining wrapper objects

execution_order.reason: Cleanup runs exactly once after the render loop exits — never inside the loop
Unbind active shader program before deletion — prevents driver use-after-free on some implementations
Unbind active VAO before deletion — ensures no VAO is active when it is freed
Unbind VBO — clean buffer state before freeing GPU buffer objects
Unbind EBO — safe unbind before deletion, though VAO already owns this binding
Restore default screen framebuffer before deleting FBOs — prevents rendering into deleted framebuffer
Unbind active texture before deletion — clean texture state before freeing texture objects

execution_order.call: ── DELETE GL RESOURCES ── (order: VAO → buffers → textures → RBO → FBO → program)
execution_order.reason: Delete child resources before parents — VAO references buffers, FBO references textures and RBOs
Delete VAO first — it holds references to VBO/EBO bindings, free it before the buffers it references
Delete vertex buffer — free GPU memory holding vertex data
Delete index buffer — free GPU memory holding index data
Delete any Uniform Buffer Objects — free shared uniform block GPU memory
Delete all scene textures — free GPU texture memory; delete before FBO color attachments
Delete FBO color attachment texture — must be detached from FBO (by deleting FBO) before or at same time
Delete depth+stencil RBO — free renderbuffer GPU memory after FBO no longer references it
Delete the FBO — automatically detaches all attachments, then frees the framebuffer object

execution_order.call: glDeleteShader(vertexShader)  // if not already deleted post-link
execution_order.reason: Delete any shader objects not cleaned up immediately after linking — safe no-op if already deleted
Delete shader program last among GL resources — frees GPU program binary and all linked shader state
Release any remaining native memory that is not managed by the JVM garbage collector
Free any STB image buffers held past upload time — native memory, not GC managed
Free any buffers allocated with MemoryUtil.memAlloc* — must be freed manually, unlike BufferUtils buffers
GLFW resources are freed after all GL resources — GL context must still exist when GL delete calls run
Free all registered GLFW window callbacks — must happen before glfwDestroyWindow or callbacks leak native memory
Destroy the window and its OpenGL context — all GL calls become invalid after this point
Release the error callback's native memory — must be the last callback freed, after glfwDestroyWindow
Shut down GLFW and free all remaining GLFW resources — must be the very last GLFW call
Hint JVM to run garbage collection — encourages collection of any remaining wrapper objects after all native resources are freed

code_example.location: Engine.java → cleanup() method, called from main() after renderLoop() exits
code_example.description: Explicit ordered teardown of every GPU and GLFW resource. Shows the correct deletion sequence: unbind all state → delete GL objects child-first → free native memory → GLFW shutdown.

integration_context.fits_in_pipeline: Phase 11 is the mirror image of Phases 1–5 in reverse. Every GPU resource allocated during init has a corresponding delete call here — VAOs, VBOs, EBOs, textures, RBOs, FBOs, and shader programs all consume GPU memory until explicitly freed. GLFW window callbacks, the error callback, and the window itself all hold native heap memory outside JVM control that the garbage collector cannot reclaim. Skipping or partially executing this phase causes GPU memory leaks, native memory leaks, and driver warnings. In long-running or reloadable engine scenarios — such as hot-reloading shaders or switching scenes — correct cleanup is what makes reinitialization safe. This phase also defines the only valid shutdown sequence: GL resources must be deleted while the context is still current, and the context is only destroyed when glfwDestroyWindow is called, making the ordering between GL deletes and GLFW teardown non-negotiable.
integration_context.comes_after: Phase 8 (Render Loop) — cleanup runs exactly once after glfwWindowShouldClose returns true and the loop exits. All GL delete calls require the OpenGL context to still be current, which it is until glfwDestroyWindow is called in this phase. Phase 10 (Framebuffer) — the FBO, its color texture, and its RBO were all created in Phase 10 and must all be deleted here. Phase 3 (Shader System) — glDeleteProgram frees the GPU program binary created during linking. Phase 4 and 5 — mesh and texture GPU memory allocated during setup is freed here.
integration_context.comes_before: Nothing — Phase 11 is the final phase. After glfwTerminate() returns, no GLFW or OpenGL calls are valid. The JVM continues running briefly after this phase to collect any remaining wrapper objects, which is why System.gc() is called as the last hint. The engine process exits cleanly after main() returns.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glfwDestroyWindow() before deleting GL resources
common_mistakes.symptom: GL delete calls after window destruction silently fail or crash — the OpenGL context is destroyed with the window, making all subsequent GL calls invalid
common_mistakes.fix: Delete all GL objects (VAOs, VBOs, textures, FBOs, shader programs) before calling glfwDestroyWindow(). The context must still be current and alive for GL delete calls to execute correctly.

common_mistakes.trap: Not calling Callbacks.glfwFreeCallbacks() before glfwDestroyWindow()
common_mistakes.symptom: Native memory leak — callback function pointers allocated by LWJGL are never freed, accumulating across engine restarts or scene reloads
common_mistakes.fix: Call Callbacks.glfwFreeCallbacks(window) before glfwDestroyWindow(window). This frees all native callback memory registered to that window in one call. Individually freeing each callback is also valid but error-prone.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling glfwTerminate() before glfwDestroyWindow()
common_mistakes.symptom: Crash or undefined behaviour — glfwTerminate() destroys all GLFW state including windows, making the subsequent glfwDestroyWindow() call operate on freed memory
common_mistakes.fix: Always call glfwDestroyWindow(window) before glfwTerminate(). The correct order is: free callbacks → destroy window → free error callback → terminate GLFW.

common_mistakes.trap: Deleting a VAO before deleting its associated VBO and EBO
common_mistakes.symptom: VBO and EBO handles become orphaned — GPU memory is leaked because the buffer objects still exist but nothing references them
common_mistakes.fix: Delete VAOs first to release their references, then delete the underlying VBOs and EBOs separately. glDeleteVertexArrays() does not automatically delete the buffers the VAO references — they must be explicitly deleted.

common_mistakes.trap: Not wrapping cleanup in a finally block in main()
common_mistakes.symptom: An exception thrown during init() or renderLoop() skips cleanup entirely — GPU memory and GLFW resources leak for the lifetime of the process
common_mistakes.fix: Wrap engine.init() and engine.renderLoop() in a try block with engine.cleanup() in the finally block. This guarantees cleanup runs even if an uncaught exception terminates the render loop early.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling stbi_image_free() on a ByteBuffer that was not allocated by stbi_load()
common_mistakes.symptom: JVM crash or heap corruption — freeing memory that was not allocated by STB causes undefined native behaviour
common_mistakes.fix: Only call STBImage.stbi_image_free() on ByteBuffers returned directly by STBImage.stbi_load(). Never call it on BufferUtils or MemoryUtil buffers — those have their own deallocation paths.

common_mistakes.trap: Not calling glDeleteShader() for shader objects that were not deleted after linking
common_mistakes.symptom: Shader object GPU memory leaks — accumulates on engines that hot-reload shaders repeatedly without deleting old shader objects
common_mistakes.fix: Track every shader ID created with glCreateShader(). If not deleted immediately after linking (via glDetachShader + glDeleteShader), delete them explicitly during shutdown. glDeleteProgram() does not delete attached shader objects.

common_mistakes.trap: Forgetting to delete the full-screen quad VAO and VBO used by the FBO pass
common_mistakes.symptom: Small but consistent GPU memory leak — quad geometry is never freed because it is not part of the main mesh list
common_mistakes.fix: Explicitly delete the quad VAO and VBO in the Framebuffer.delete() method alongside the FBO, color texture, and RBO. Every glGen* call must have a corresponding glDelete* call at shutdown.

common_mistakes.trap: Calling MemoryUtil.memFree() on a buffer allocated with BufferUtils.createFloatBuffer()
common_mistakes.symptom: JVM crash or native heap corruption — BufferUtils buffers are managed differently from MemoryUtil buffers and must not be freed with memFree()
common_mistakes.fix: Only call MemoryUtil.memFree() on buffers allocated with MemoryUtil.memAlloc*, memAllocFloat*, etc. Buffers from BufferUtils.createFloatBuffer() are GC-managed and must not be manually freed. Never mix allocation and deallocation APIs.

pipeline_overview.name: Window & Context Creation
pipeline_overview.summary: Initialize GLFW, configure window hints, create OS window and OpenGL context, register input callbacks, make context current, enable VSync, show window.
pipeline_overview.outputs: A valid long window handle and an active OpenGL context on the calling thread.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: glfwMakeContextCurrent() succeeds — nothing GL-related is valid before this.

pipeline_overview.name: OpenGL Context Initialization
pipeline_overview.summary: Load all GL function pointers, log driver version, set viewport, clear color, enable depth testing, face culling, and default polygon fill mode.
pipeline_overview.outputs: A fully initialised GL state with correct default settings ready for resource creation.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: GL.createCapabilities() completes — no GL call is safe before this.

pipeline_overview.name: Shader System
pipeline_overview.summary: Load GLSL source from disk, compile vertex and fragment shaders, link into a program, validate, delete shader objects, activate program, cache all uniform locations.
pipeline_overview.outputs: A linked and active shader program with all uniform locations cached in fields.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: glLinkProgram() succeeds — uniform locations and draw calls require a linked program.

pipeline_overview.name: Vertex Buffer & Mesh Setup
pipeline_overview.summary: Generate VAO, VBO, EBO. Upload vertex and index data to GPU. Configure vertex attribute pointers for position, UV, and normals. Unbind to seal state.
pipeline_overview.outputs: A fully configured VAO ready to be bound and drawn in the render loop.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: VAO is unbound after setup — do not begin the render loop until all meshes are uploaded.

pipeline_overview.name: Texture Loading & Binding
pipeline_overview.summary: Flip image Y axis, load file from disk with STBImage, generate texture object, set sampling parameters, upload pixel data to GPU, generate mipmaps, free CPU buffer.
pipeline_overview.outputs: A GPU texture object ready to be bound to a texture unit before each draw call.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: All textures must be uploaded before the render loop begins.

pipeline_overview.name: Camera & Transformation Matrices
pipeline_overview.location: engine.init() for allocation and projection — engine.renderLoop() for view and model rebuild each frame
pipeline_overview.summary: Pre-allocate model, view, projection matrices and upload buffer. Build projection once. Each frame: update camera from input, rebuild view with lookAt, rebuild model with TRS per object.
pipeline_overview.outputs: Three matrices per frame (model, view, projection) written into a FloatBuffer ready for uniform upload.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: Matrices must be fully built before glUniformMatrix4fv() is called each frame.

pipeline_overview.name: Uniform Management
pipeline_overview.location: After glLinkProgram() in init() for caching — at the top of each frame in renderLoop() for uploading
pipeline_overview.summary: Cache all uniform locations once after linking. Each frame: activate program, upload matrices, light data, time, and texture unit assignments before draw calls.
pipeline_overview.outputs: All shader inputs populated with current frame data before the draw call executes.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: All uniforms must be uploaded before glDrawElements() — draw calls read whatever is currently set.

pipeline_overview.name: Render Loop
pipeline_overview.location: engine.renderLoop() — runs every frame until glfwWindowShouldClose returns true
pipeline_overview.summary: Per-frame sequence: compute deltaTime, poll events, update logic, clear buffers, bind shader, upload uniforms, draw opaque objects, draw transparent objects sorted back-to-front, swap buffers.
pipeline_overview.outputs: One completed rendered frame presented to the screen per iteration.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: Loop runs indefinitely until window close is requested.

pipeline_overview.name: Depth Testing & Blending
pipeline_overview.location: engine.init() for default state — engine.renderLoop() as pass-level state switches between opaque, transparent, and stencil passes
pipeline_overview.summary: Opaque pass: depth writes on, blending off. Transparent pass: sort back-to-front, depth writes off, blending on. Optional stencil pass: write mask then read mask for outline effects.
pipeline_overview.outputs: Correctly composited frame with opaque occlusion, sorted transparency, and optional stencil masking.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: Opaque pass must fully complete before transparent pass begins each frame.

pipeline_overview.name: Framebuffer & Offscreen Rendering
pipeline_overview.location: engine.init() for FBO creation — engine.renderLoop() wraps the entire scene draw in a two-pass structure
pipeline_overview.summary: Create FBO with color texture and depth RBO, validate completeness. Each frame: bind FBO and render scene into it (pass 1), restore screen framebuffer and draw full-screen quad through post-process shader sampling the FBO color texture (pass 2).
pipeline_overview.outputs: A post-processed frame composited from the FBO color texture and presented to the screen.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: FBO must be validated complete before the render loop starts.

pipeline_overview.name: Resource Cleanup & Shutdown
pipeline_overview.location: engine.cleanup() — called from main() finally block after renderLoop() exits
pipeline_overview.summary: Unbind all active GL state, delete all GPU resources in child-before-parent order (VAO → buffers → textures → RBO → FBO → program), free native CPU memory, free GLFW callbacks, destroy window, terminate GLFW.
pipeline_overview.outputs: All GPU memory freed, GLFW terminated, process exits cleanly with no leaks.
pipeline_overview.blocks_until: All GL deletes must complete before glfwDestroyWindow() — the context is invalid after that call.

minimal_working_structure.description: A minimal Java class structure showing exactly where each phase's code lives. Three entry methods — init(), setup(), renderLoop() — map directly to the pipeline phases. cleanup() is included as the required fourth method called from the finally block in main().

classes.responsibility: Entry point. Owns the engine lifecycle — constructs Engine, drives the four-phase sequence, guarantees cleanup runs via finally.
Central coordinator. Owns the window handle, render loop, and references to all subsystems. Delegates to ShaderProgram, Mesh, Texture, Camera, Framebuffer.
Compiles and links GLSL shaders. Exposes bind(), unbind(), delete(), and getProgramID(). Used in setup() and renderLoop().
Owns VAO, VBO, EBO. Uploads geometry in constructor. Exposes draw() and delete(). Used in setup() and renderLoop().
Loads image with STBImage, uploads to GPU, manages parameters and mipmaps. Exposes bind(unit), unbind(), delete(). Used in setup() and renderLoop().
Owns camera position, direction, matrices. Exposes update(window, deltaTime), getViewMatrix(), getProjectionMatrix(), buildProjection(aspect). Used in setup() and renderLoop().
Caches uniform locations after linking. Provides typed setters (setMatrix4f, setVec3, setFloat, setInt). Used in setup() for caching and renderLoop() for uploads.
Creates and validates FBO with color texture and depth RBO. Owns the full-screen quad. Exposes bindForRendering(), drawToScreen(shaderID), delete(). Used in setup() and renderLoop().
